title: Dental Technician
how_to_become: To become a dental technician, you'll need to complete a pre-registration training programme and then register with the General Dental Council (GDC).

salary.entry_note: Dental technicians in the NHS start at £33,247 before tax. Trainees are paid a percentage of the qualified salary, increasing as they progress through training.
salary.experienced_note: Specialist dental technicians can earn up to £50,702. Advanced dental technicians can earn up to £59,159 before tax.

description: Dental technicians are essential members of the dental team who manufacture, repair, and provide advice on custom-made dental appliances prescribed by a dentist or clinical dental technician. They use a wide range of materials, specialised equipment, and cutting-edge technologies like CAD/CAM, 3D printing, and digital imaging.
role_overview: As a dental technician, you'll work within a team with a growing emphasis on innovative digital technologies to design and produce customised dental devices. Work is carried out in regulated dental laboratories in commercial, independent, or hospital settings.
specialist_areas: Fixed prosthesis — crowns, bridges, and veneers
Orthodontics — appliances that correct teeth alignment and jaw positioning
Maxillofacial prosthesis — specialised prosthetics for patients with facial or oral defects due to trauma, surgery, or congenital conditions

entry_requirements.overview: A pre-registration training programme leading to GDC registration is required. English language proficiency is required by the GDC.
entry_requirements.academic_requirements: 2 SQA Highers at grade C or above, one in a science subject which could include Maths, or equivalent qualifications at SCQF level 6 or above.

work_experience: Work placements and volunteering can provide helpful experience when applying for NHS roles.

education_and_training.programme: University of Aberdeen Diploma in Higher Education (DipHE) in Dental Technology at SCQF Level 8.
education_and_training.duration: 2 years, studying towards a professional qualification while working as a trainee dental technician in a registered dental lab.
programme_covers: Technical and theoretical skills for creating, repairing, and advising on custom dental devices
Technical and digital methods for designing fixed and removable prostheses and orthodontic devices
Anatomy, physiology, and human diseases relevant to dental technology
education_and_training.english_language: All dental professionals must be sufficiently fluent in written and spoken English to communicate effectively with patients, relatives, the dental team, and other healthcare professionals.

extended_scope: Working with a dentist in the clinic assisting with treatment by helping to fit attachments at the chairside
Carrying out intra-oral and extra-oral tracing
Carrying out intra-oral scanning for CAD/CAM

career_progression.overview: With experience and qualifications, dental technicians can move into new career paths or registrant groups.
progression_roles: Healthcare scientist specialising in reconstructive science

professional_bodies: General Dental Council (GDC) — mandatory for NHS practice

chunks.text: To become a dental technician, complete a pre-registration training programme and register with the General Dental Council (GDC).
Dental technicians in the NHS start at £33,247 before tax. Specialist dental technicians can earn up to £50,702. Advanced dental technicians can earn up to £59,159 before tax.

Dental technicians manufacture, repair, and advise on custom-made dental appliances prescribed by a dentist or clinical dental technician, using materials, specialised equipment, and technologies including CAD/CAM, 3D printing, and digital imaging.

Dental technicians work in four specialist areas: removable prosthodontics (dentures), fixed prosthesis (crowns, bridges, and veneers), orthodontics (teeth alignment and jaw positioning appliances), and maxillofacial prosthesis (prosthetics for patients with facial or oral defects).

Useful school subjects include Biology, Design and Manufacture, English, and Maths. Academic entry requires 2 SQA Highers at grade C or above including a science subject, or equivalent at SCQF level 6 or above. English language proficiency is required by the GDC.

The University of Aberdeen offers a 2-year Diploma in Higher Education (DipHE) in Dental Technology at SCQF Level 8, covering technical and digital methods for creating custom dental devices, biomaterials, anatomy, physiology, and relevant human diseases.

Dental technicians can extend their scope to include chairside work with dentists, taking impressions, recording facebows, intra-oral scanning for CAD/CAM, and taking intra and extra-oral photographs.
With experience and qualifications, dental technicians can progress to clinical dental technician, management, teaching, or healthcare scientist specialising in reconstructive science.
Dental technicians must register with the General Dental Council (GDC). They can also join the Dental Technologists Association (DTA).
